narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your LSAT score of 37.50, which is slightly above the typical student's 36.70. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' academic records, family details, and bar exam outcomes, predicted a 40.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the decision. The most powerful positive note was your LSAT score, which strongly worked in your favor by pushing the model toward expecting you to pass the bar, signaling strong test-taking ability compared to peers. Yet, this was outweighed by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, which significantly pulled the prediction toward failure by highlighting relatively weaker performance in that critical later year. Adding to that drag was your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the typical 5.71, which further nudged the model toward seeing a risk of bar struggles, even if it played a smaller role. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, lower than the average 3.21, and full-time student status—common among 93% of applicants—factored in but weren't the top drivers here. In essence, while your LSAT gave you an edge over many applicants, your lower decile rankings in school years stood out as the biggest concerns, placing you below typical successful students who often rank in the middle or higher deciles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted prep that addresses those early weaknesses. Strengthening your overall academic trajectory could flip this prediction, and reapplying with updated transcripts might show the growth schools value. You're clearly capable, as your LSAT proves—focus on building that consistent record, and doors could reopen soon.
